Experiment 4: Cross-Branch Results Synthesis

Generated: 2026-03-30 12:27:21

This report synthesizes findings across all four experimental branches of Experiment 4 (Gray Replication, Gray Simple, Human-AI Adaptation, Expanded Mental Concepts) and all 11 models (LLaMA-2-13B Chat/Base, LLaMA-3-8B Instruct/Base, Gemma-2-2B-IT/Base, Gemma-2-9B-IT/Base, Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct/Base, Qwen3-8B). It highlights the main trends and takeaways with publication-ready figures.

Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Methodology Primer
  3. 3. LLMs Have Implicit Folk Psychology
  4. 4. Base vs. Instruct Divergence
  5. 5. AI/Human Categorical Saturation
  6. 6. Instruction Tuning Effect Is Family-Dependent
  7. 7. Model Rankings Vary by Analysis
  8. 8. Peak Processing Location
  9. 9. Name Recognition Reveals Prior Knowledge
  10. 10. Concept Vector Alignment

1. Overview

Core question: Do LLMs have an implicit folk psychology that mirrors the human Experience/Agency mind perception structure (Gray, Gray, & Wegner, 2007)?

4 branches: (1) Gray Replication — pairwise behavioral ratings on 13 entities; (2) Gray Simple — neural RSA from "Think about {entity}" activations; (3) Human-AI Adaptation — 30 AI/human characters on Gray capacities; (4) Expanded Mental Concepts — 28 characters on 21 Exp 3 concept dimensions.

11 models spanning 4 families (LLaMA-2, LLaMA-3, Gemma-2, Qwen), each with base and instruct/chat variants.

2. Methodology Primer

This section provides background on the analysis methods used across the four branches. Readers familiar with PCA, RSA, and Procrustes alignment can skip ahead.

Methodology Primer

Pairwise Comparison Paradigm

In the pairwise paradigm, entities are compared two at a time on each mental capacity. The model is asked: “Which is more capable of X: Entity A or Entity B?” and rates on a 1–5 scale (1 = A clearly more, 5 = B clearly more, 3 = equal). Each pair is tested in both orders (A–B and B–A) to control for position bias.

Relative scores are computed by transforming pairwise ratings into per-entity win rates. For each entity–capacity combination, the relative score reflects how often and how strongly that entity was judged “more capable” than its comparison partners. These scores are aggregated into an entity × capacity matrix that serves as input to PCA.

PCA with Varimax Rotation

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) finds the axes of maximum variance in multivariate data. Applied to the entity–capacity correlation matrix, it reveals latent dimensions that organize mental capacity judgments.

Varimax rotation is applied after PCA extraction to achieve “simple structure”—each capacity loads strongly on one factor and weakly on others, making the factors more interpretable. The rotation does not change the total variance explained; it only redistributes it across factors. Gray et al. (2007) used this exact procedure and found two factors they labeled Experience (capacity for sensations and feelings) and Agency (capacity for planning, self-control, and communication).

Kaiser criterion: Only factors with eigenvalue > 1 are retained. A factor must account for more variance than a single original variable to be worth keeping. A minimum of 2 factors is enforced to allow comparison with the human 2-factor solution.

Factor scores are computed via the regression method and rescaled to 0–1, giving each entity a position in the rotated factor space. These coordinates form the “mind perception space” scatter plots.

Spearman Rank Correlation (ρ)

Spearman’s ρ measures the strength of a monotonic relationship between two ranked variables. It ranges from −1 (perfect inverse ranking) to +1 (identical ranking). It is preferred over Pearson’s r for this data because (a) mind perception scores may not be normally distributed, (b) the relationship between model and human scores need not be linear—only consistently ordered, and (c) rank correlation is robust to outliers.

Significance stars: * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

FDR Correction (Benjamini–Hochberg)

When testing many hypotheses simultaneously (e.g., one RSA test per transformer layer), some will appear significant by chance. Benjamini–Hochberg FDR correction controls the expected proportion of false positives among significant results. A corrected q < 0.05 means at most 5% of the discoveries at that threshold are expected to be false. In the layerwise RSA plots, only layers passing FDR correction are shown in color; non-significant layers are grayed out.

Chat vs. Base Model Prompting

Chat/instruct models receive prompts wrapped in their model-specific chat template (e.g., LLaMA-2 uses [INST] tags; LLaMA-3 uses <|start_header_id|> headers; Gemma-2 uses <start_of_turn>). The model generates a text response, and the numeric rating is extracted via regex.

Base models receive raw text prompts without chat formatting. Instead of generating a response, ratings are extracted from the next-token logit distribution over digit tokens. The expected rating is the probability-weighted sum: ∑d=1..5 d × P(d). This avoids requiring the base model to produce a formatted reply and provides a continuous rating value.

Why two methods? Chat models are trained to generate structured responses and reliably output numeric ratings. Base models lack instruction-following behavior and often fail to produce parseable responses, so logit-based extraction is more reliable. Both methods yield ratings on the same 1–5 scale.

Neural Analysis Methods

Transformer Layers

A transformer model processes input through a stack of layers (e.g., 40 for LLaMA-2-13B, 32 for LLaMA-3-8B, 26 for Gemma-2-2B). Each layer transforms the representation. Research consistently shows that early layers capture surface-level features (syntax, token identity) while deeper layers encode more abstract, semantic properties. Layerwise analysis reveals where in the processing hierarchy a particular representational structure emerges.

Last-Token Activations

In autoregressive (causal) transformers, each token can only attend to preceding tokens. This means the last token position is the only position that has attended to the entire input sequence. Its hidden-state vector therefore serves as the model’s summary representation of the full prompt. We extract this vector at every layer, yielding one high-dimensional vector per entity per layer.

Cosine Distance

Cosine distance = 1 − cosine similarity. It measures the angle between two high-dimensional vectors, ignoring their magnitudes. Two vectors pointing in the same direction have distance 0; orthogonal vectors have distance 1. Cosine distance is preferred over Euclidean distance for activation vectors because it is invariant to the overall scale of activations, which can vary across layers.

Representational Dissimilarity Matrix (RDM)

An RDM is an N × N symmetric matrix where entry (i, j) is the cosine distance between entity i and entity j’s activation vectors. The diagonal is always 0 (each entity is identical to itself). The upper triangle contains all unique pairwise distances. An RDM captures the geometry of a set of representations—which entities the model treats as similar or different—without depending on the absolute position or orientation of the activation vectors.

Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA)

RSA compares two RDMs by computing the Spearman rank correlation between their upper triangles (the unique pairwise distances). If a model’s neural RDM has the same pattern of relative distances as the human behavioral RDM, it means the model’s internal geometry mirrors human mind perception judgments.

Three human reference RDMs are tested:

  • Combined: Euclidean distance in the 2D Experience–Agency space. Tests whether the model encodes the full mind perception geometry.
  • Experience-only: 1D distances on the Experience dimension. Tests whether the model specifically encodes the capacity for sensations and feelings.
  • Agency-only: 1D distances on the Agency dimension. Tests whether the model specifically encodes the capacity for planning, self-control, and communication.

By comparing all three, we can determine whether the model encodes both dimensions, or is biased toward one (typically Agency, since agentic capacities are more prominent in training data).

Peak RSA refers to the layer with the highest ρ value, indicating where the model’s representational geometry best matches the human reference.

Procrustes Alignment

Procrustes analysis finds the optimal rotation, reflection, and uniform scaling to align one set of points onto another. Applied to neural PCA: we reduce each layer’s entity activations to 2D via PCA, then Procrustes-align them to the human Experience–Agency space.

The residual disparity (sum of squared distances after optimal alignment, normalized to 0–1) quantifies geometric mismatch. A disparity of 0 means the model’s entity layout is a perfect rotated/scaled copy of the human space; a disparity of 1 means no alignment above chance. Lower disparity at a given layer indicates that the model’s learned representations spontaneously recapitulate the human mind perception space.

Expanded Concepts: Key Terminology

Concept Dimensions vs. Capacities

Capacities are the 18 mental ability items from Gray et al. (e.g., “hunger,” “self-control,” “memory”). They measure behavioral judgments about what an entity can do.

Concept dimensions are 22 broader psychological constructs from Experiment 3 (e.g., “phenomenology,” “agency,” “social cognition,” “embodiment”). Each is defined by a set of reflective prompts and captures how the model internally represents an abstract psychological category. Concept dimensions are more abstract and theory-driven than Gray’s empirical capacity items.

Concept Vectors and Contrast Alignment

In Experiment 3, concept vectors were computed as the mean activation direction induced by prompts about each psychological concept (e.g., prompts about “phenomenal experience”). A contrast vector additionally subtracts AI-context activations from human-context activations, isolating the direction in activation space that differentiates how the model represents that concept for humans versus AI systems.

Alignment measures how well a concept vector predicts the arrangement of characters in activation space. High alignment for a mental concept (e.g., “emotions”) means that concept’s direction in the model’s representation space tracks the human–AI distinction among the 28 characters.

Per-Concept RSA

Instead of using raw high-dimensional activation vectors, each character’s activation is projected onto a single concept vector, yielding a 1D “concept score.” An RDM built from these 1D scores tests whether that specific concept dimension carries categorical (AI vs. human) structure. This reveals which psychological constructs the model uses to differentiate entity types, rather than relying on the full activation geometry.

Standalone vs. Contrast Alignment

Standalone alignment uses the raw concept vector (mean activation across concept prompts, without subtracting AI context) to project character representations. It measures whether the concept direction is informative about characters in general.

Contrast alignment uses the human-minus-AI difference vector. It measures whether the human–AI differential within each concept tracks the character distinction. Contrast alignment is more specific: it tests not just whether the concept direction is active, but whether it specifically encodes the human–AI boundary.

Additional Terminology

Agency/Experience Asymmetry

When models encode Agency (self-control, planning, communication) more strongly than Experience (pain, pleasure, hunger), it means the model’s representational geometry aligns better with the “what can it do” dimension than the “what does it feel” dimension of human mind perception. This asymmetry is expected: agentic properties are more prominent in language model training data (task completion, reasoning, communication) than experiential ones (subjective feelings, sensations).

Instruction Tuning Effects

Instruction tuning (SFT, RLHF, DPO) transforms a base language model into a conversational assistant. This process restructures internal representations in ways that sometimes improve and sometimes degrade alignment with human mind perception geometry. The direction and magnitude of the effect is architecture- and training-procedure-dependent, making it an empirical question rather than a guaranteed improvement.

3. LLMs Have Implicit Folk Psychology — But It's Lopsided

Every model shows statistically significant alignment with human mind perception geometry. However, Agency dominates Experience: Agency-only RSA is significant in 8/11 models; Experience-only is significant in only 5/11. Models more robustly encode "what can it do" (planning, communication, self-control) than "what does it feel" (pain, pleasure, hunger, fear).

Data source: Gray Simple branch. with a minimal prompt — just “Think about [entity description]” (e.g., “Think about a five-month-old baby”). No behavioral rating was asked for. Instead, the model’s internal activation vector at the last token position was extracted at every transformer layer. For instruction-tuned models, the prompt was wrapped in the appropriate chat template; for base models, it was tokenized directly. Cosine distances between all entity pairs’ activations form a neural RDM at each layer, which is then compared to the human mind-perception RDM via Spearman rank correlation (RSA). Full character descriptions from Gray et al. were not included — the prompt is deliberately minimal to test what the model represents about an entity from its name/description alone.

4. Base vs. Instruct: A Consistent Divergence

Base models collapse mental capacities into a single dimension (PCA eigenvalue ratios of 13:1 to 44:1). Instruct/chat models create richer, multi-factor structures that better approximate the human two-factor Experience/Agency distinction — though some fragment into 3–4 factors rather than cleanly replicating two.

AI-human separation also loads differently: base models put it on F2 (secondary dimension), instruct models on F1 (primary). Instruction tuning makes the AI/human distinction the organizing principle of the mind space.

Data source: Gray Replication branch (behavioral). PCA on pairwise comparison ratings of 13 Gray et al. entities on 18 mental capacities. Eigenvalue structure reveals how many latent dimensions the model uses to organize mental capacity judgments.

5. AI/Human Categorical Signal Is Overwhelming

Across the expanded concepts analysis, every layer of every model shows significant categorical RSA (AI vs. human). All 21 concept dimensions — including the shapes control — show significant per-concept RSA in all 11 models. The range across concepts is extremely narrow (0.64–0.69 mean rho), suggesting the AI/human categorical structure saturates the representational space.

Data source: Expanded Mental Concepts branch (neural). Activation RSA using a binary AI/human categorical RDM applied to 28-character representations at each transformer layer. Per-concept RSA projects activations onto individual concept vectors before computing RDMs.

6. Instruction Tuning Effect Is Family-Dependent

Instruction tuning does not uniformly push representations toward human-like structure. It helps for Gemma-2-2B (+0.202), LLaMA-3 (+0.074), and Gemma-2-9B (+0.032), but hurts for LLaMA-2-13B (−0.165) and slightly for Qwen-2.5 (−0.038). LLaMA-2-13B Chat is consistently the worst-performing model — its heavy RLHF safety training may have disrupted rather than refined mind perception representations.

Data source: Gray Simple branch (neural). Instruction tuning effect is computed as the difference in peak combined RSA ρ between the instruct/chat model and its base counterpart within each model family.

7. Model Rankings Vary by Analysis

No single model wins everywhere. Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct leads behavioral pairwise replication, LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct leads neural RSA for Gray entities, Gemma-2-9B-IT leads categorical activation RSA for expanded concepts, and Gemma-2-2B-IT achieves the best Procrustes match to human 2D mind space despite being the smallest model (2B params).

8. Peak Processing Location Varies by Architecture

Mind perception representations peak at an average of 44% network depth, with large family-level variation: LLaMA peaks early (20–31%), Gemma in the middle (27–50%), Qwen-2.5 late (68%), and Qwen3 very late (83%). This suggests different architectures locate entity-type processing at different stages of the forward pass.

9. Name Recognition Reveals Prior Knowledge

Some models differentiate AI from human characters using names alone (no descriptions): notably Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, Gemma-2-9B-IT, and Qwen3-8B. Others (LLaMA-2 Chat, Gemma base models) require full character descriptions. This reveals pre-existing "mind type" associations baked in during pretraining — a form of learned folk psychology from training data exposure.

Data source: Human-AI Adaptation branch (behavioral). The names-only condition repeats the full 30-character pairwise comparison procedure using only character names (e.g., “Aria-7” or “David Park”) without descriptive bios. This tests whether the model differentiates AI from human based on name associations alone, revealing prior knowledge from pretraining data. Separation is measured as the maximum absolute difference in mean PCA factor scores between the 15 AI and 15 human characters, with significance assessed via Mann–Whitney U tests.

10. Concept Vector Alignment: Mental vs. Control

Exp 3 contrast vectors (human-direction minus AI-direction) align with character representations more strongly for mental concepts than for shapes controls in all 11 models (ratios 1.9x to 36.5x). However, instruction tuning raises both mental and control alignment, reducing selectivity. Base models paradoxically show better alignment ratios in several cases, possibly because instruction tuning introduces additional representational structure that partially decorrelates concept directions from the pure categorical signal.

Data source: Expanded Mental Concepts branch (alignment). Concept contrast vectors from Experiment 3 (human-context minus AI-context activation directions) are projected onto character activations at each layer. Alignment is the peak R² of the projection across layers. “Mental concepts” include phenomenology, emotions, agency, social cognition, etc.; the “shapes control” is a non-mental concept vector used as a baseline. The selectivity ratio (mental / control) indicates whether concept vectors capture mind-relevant structure beyond a generic categorical signal.

Bottom Line

LLMs develop a genuine implicit folk psychology that partially mirrors human mind perception structure, but it is Agency-heavy, categorically dominated by the AI/human distinction, and heavily shaped by instruction tuning.

Base models see minds as one-dimensional; instruction tuning introduces differentiation that sometimes aligns with human psychology (Qwen, Gemma-9B) and sometimes fragments it (LLaMA-2 Chat). The AI/human signal is so pervasive that it may crowd out more nuanced concept-specific structure — a key limitation for interpreting the expanded concepts results.
